title: The Most Powerful 400 Words In Teaching – Transforming Learning Through Teaching – From the LCC Center for Teaching Excellence
excerpts:
Section Title: About
Content:
Reading Time: 2 minutes
In Spring 2015, Lansing Community College faculty participated in a Gratitude Project, sharing something about their jobs for which they were grateful. One submission stood out from the rest. Professor Chris Manning talked about a student who taught him exactly why community colleges exist.
Using Professor Manning’s piece as an inspiration, [Transforming Learning Through Teaching (TLTT)](https://www.lcc.edu/cte/workshops/transforming-learning.html) participants now complete a short essay to inspire our fellow teachers and remind us why community colleges exist and why we choose to teach at a community college. The TLTT course is taught through the [Center for Teaching Excellence](https://www.lcc.edu/cte/) .

title: Christopher Manning: Linguistics and the Development of NLP
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Share this post
Content:
**Have suggestions for future podcast guests (or other feedback)? Let us know [here](https://forms.gle/jAtWTh4qheASVcDp7) !**
In episode 41 of The Gradient Podcast, [Daniel Bashir](https://twitter.com/spaniel_bashir) speaks to [Christopher Manning](https://twitter.com/chrmanning?lang=en) .
Chris is the Director of the Stanford AI Lab and an Associate Director of the Stanford Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence Institute. He is an ACM Fellow, an AAAI Fellow, and past President of ACL. His work currently focuses on applying deep learning to natural language processing; it has included tree recursive neural networks, GloVe, neural machine translation, and computational linguistic approaches to parsing, among other topics.

excerpts:
Section Title: ... > [Community events celebrating open research and scholarship](https://www.ukcorr.org/2025/11...
Content:
[christopher.manning@lshtm.ac.uk](https://www.ukcorr.org/author/christopher-manninglshtm-ac-uk/) [Uncategorized](https://www.ukcorr.org/category/uncategorized/)
Looking back at the blog it’s been a bit quiet of late, with the last post way back in November 2022…I don’t think this is a reflection of a lack of community around open research and repositories and the mailing list is still pretty active. Rather everyone is busy…and perhaps blogs are a bit old…
